#  Polarization Behaviour

The polarization–electric field (P–E) loops of the Cd3 ceramic are remarkably slim, approaching a nearly linear shape with negligible remnant polarization and minimal hysteresis (Fig. 1c). This slimming is a direct consequence of the heterogeneous dipolar structures (HDSs) revealed by phase-field simulations and atomic-scale imaging: the disordered modulation disrupts long-range AFE ordering, creating nanoscale local heterogeneity that enables rapid polarization reversal upon field removal. However, it is critical to question whether such slim loops alone constitute a sufficient condition to classify the material as a “relaxor” or “relaxor ferroelectric.” In canonical relaxor ferroelectrics, slim loops arise from polar nanoregions (PNRs) that exhibit strong frequency dispersion and a broad, frequency-dependent dielectric maximum. Here, the slim loops in Cd3 are instead rooted in the dynamic, locally heterogeneous HDSs within an AFE matrix—not in the formation of PNRs. The dielectric data (Fig. 4a) show three distinct phase transitions (AFEI→AFEII, AFEII→MCC, MCC→PE) with no evidence of a diffuse, frequency-dependent peak characteristic of relaxor behavior. Thus, while the slim P–E loops superficially resemble those of relaxors, the underlying mechanism is fundamentally different: HDSs suppress hysteresis by enabling rapid, reversible switching of AFE nanodomains, not by freezing out PNR dynamics. The term “relaxor” should therefore be reserved for systems where slim loops are accompanied by canonical relaxor signatures; in this work, the slim loops are a signature of HDS-driven disorder and short-range order, not relaxor ferroelectricity.
